name: Router5
description: Framework-agnostic, state-machine-style router with a React binding

description.value: Flexible and powerful universal routing solution
description.comment: GitHub tagline. A view-agnostic router that processes routing instructions and outputs state updates; framework bindings (react-router5, redux-router5) consume that state.

category.comment: A drop-in, view-agnostic routing engine you add to an existing app; not a build/server framework. The React binding (react-router5) layers on top.

active-maintenance.comment: Last repository push was 2023-01-07 and the last npm release (8.0.1) was 2020-03-19 — no commits or releases in the last ~6 months as of 2026-07-14. Snyk classifies the package as Inactive.

initial-release.comment: Repository created 2015-06-29; first public releases in 2015.

latest-release.comment: router5 8.0.1, published 2020-03-19; still the latest version as of 2026-07-14. The repo publishes no GitHub Releases, so the npm publish date is used.

github-stars.comment: Stars on router5/router5, checked 2026-07-14 via GitHub API.

npm-weekly-downloads.comment: Last-week downloads of the core `router5` package (2026-07-07 to 2026-07-13). The React binding `react-router5` adds ~6,647/week.

routing-paradigm.comment: Routes are declared as plain config objects with name/path (optionally nested as a tree). The React binding (react-router5 v8) exposes useRoute/useRouter hooks plus a RouteNode component to consume router state; there is no component-based route declaration or file-based routing.

nested-layout-routes.comment: Routes can be nested into a tree with names relative to their parent; react-router5's RouteNode component renders per active route segment, enabling shared parent layouts.

dynamic-params.comment: URL path params via the :param syntax, e.g. /users/:id.

optional-catch-all-params.comment: Supports splat parameters (*splat) spanning multiple segments plus matrix (;param) and optional query (?param) parameters.

search-params-api.comment: First-class query-params handling with configurable arrayFormat, booleanFormat and nullFormat serialization options.

programmatic-navigation.comment: Imperative navigation via router.navigate(routeName, routeParams, opts).

route-guards-middleware.comment: Per-segment canActivate/canDeactivate lifecycle guards plus a global middleware pipeline and plugin system for transition side effects.

written-in-typescript.comment: All packages were rewritten in TypeScript as of v7 and ship first-class type definitions.

type-safe-paths-links.comment: Routes are referenced by plain string names (router.navigate('users.view')); names are not checked against the defined route tree.

type-safe-params.comment: Params are typed as a generic Record; there is no per-route inference of param names or types at usage sites.

type-safe-search-params.comment: Query params share the same untyped generic params object; no schema-based typing or validation.

codegen-required.comment: No route-type generation step exists; types are hand-written generics rather than inferred or generated.

type-safety-rating.comment: TS type definitions exist (v7 rewrite) but route names, path params, and query params are not checked against the route configuration — matches rubric level 2.

route-data-loaders.comment: No loader/useLoaderData-style primitive that fetches data before render and hands it to the view. Data loading is DIY inside canActivate/middleware; the router only outputs route state.

actions-mutations.comment: No built-in action/mutation or form-handling primitive tied to routes.

prefetching.comment: No route code/data prefetching on hover or intent.

pending-transition-ui.comment: Exposes transition start/success/error events, but ships no pending-state UI or React useTransition integration.

scroll-restoration.comment: No built-in scroll restoration across navigations; left to the application.

per-route-code-splitting.comment: No native per-route lazy loading; splitting is DIY with React.lazy on your components.

per-route-error-handling.comment: Transition errors surface via events/callbacks, but there are no route-scoped error elements or error boundaries.

ssr-support.comment: Router is universal — the same route logic runs client- and server-side, and there is a dedicated universal-routing guide. React rendering (renderToString/hydrate) is DIY, not turnkey.

react-server-components.comment: No RSC support; the library predates React Server Components and is a client/universal state router.

client-only-usable.comment: The browser plugin enables a pure client-side SPA router with no server required.

framework-bound.comment: Drop-in library with no build/server toolchain of its own; view-agnostic core with optional React/Redux/etc. bindings.

bundle-size.comment: Minified + gzipped size of the core router5@8.0.1 per Bundlephobia (9,280 bytes; ~30 KB minified). The react-router5 binding is a separate small package.

zero-runtime-dependencies.comment: Depends on three runtime packages: route-node, router5-transition-path, and symbol-observable.

tree-shakeable.comment: Package reports hasSideEffects: false, enabling dead-code elimination.

documentation-quality.comment: Dedicated docs site with guides (routing, middleware, plugins, universal routing, preventing navigation), integration guides (React, Redux), migration guides, and a full API reference with examples. Not a 5 (no interactive playground).

official-devtools.comment: Ships a loggerPlugin that logs transitions to the console, but no dedicated devtools/inspector UI.

data-fetching-integration.comment: No first-class data-fetching integration; data loading is done DIY inside middleware/canActivate. Official bindings exist for Redux/observables (state, not data-fetching), which are outside the defined tag set.

predecessor-lineage.comment: Independent project with no predecessor router.
